writing_points.claim: For instance, controllers that use thermal sensor feedback to maintain a stable melt pool size, thereby preventing overheating or LoF.
writing_points.source_text: Future PBF and DED machines will increasingly incorporate feedback mechanisms that adjust process parameters in real time to avoid or fix defects as they form. The ultimate vision is an autonomous system where the printing process can “heal” itself for example, by pausing and re-melting a problematic region, modifying the laser power or scan speed on the fly, or changing the powder feed rate in DED if a flaw is detected. Initial steps toward this vision are already evident in research. For instance, controllers that use thermal sensor feedback to maintain a stable melt pool size, thereby preventing overheating or LoF. Another example is the use of AE or plasma spectroscopy signals to detect a developing keyhole or crack and then immediately modulate the energy input to mitigate it. However, implementing true defect correction in real time faces challenges, including the need for ultra-fast data processing and actuation as well as strategies to distinguish false alarms from true defect indications. DED processes, with their flexible toolpaths, offer a testbed for such adaptive control (e.g., steering a robotic deposition head to fill a void or adjusting wire feed to eliminate a pore), whereas in PBF the high scan speeds and multi-laser coordination make intervention more complex. Ongoing research is addressing these issues, and it is anticipated that future machines will routinely include a closed-loop control layer. The ability to respond to dynamic changes in the melt pool or build environment and automatically tune parameters can revolutionize AM by drastically reducing defect.
8.4. Scalability and Standardization for Industrial Deployment

writing_points.claim: For in situ monitoring to transition from research labs to widespread industrial use, scalability and standardization are paramount.
writing_points.source_text: For in situ monitoring to transition from research labs to widespread industrial use, scalability and standardization are paramount. One aspect is the scalability of data handling and computation. Future monitoring systems must manage with massive data throughput from high-resolution, multi-sensor setups, especially in modern AM machines. This is driving the development of scalable software architectures (such as cloud or edge computing pipelines) that can process and analyze sensor data in parallel and in real time. There is also movement toward standardized metrics for defect detection performance and part quality indicators to consistently evaluate monitoring system effectiveness. Industrial and regulatory bodies (e.g., ASTM, ISO) are beginning to discuss norms for digital quality records derived from in situ monitoring, which could eventually feed into certification of critical AM parts. Achieving this would allow results from different sites to be compared and combined, accelerating learning. In summary, the future will see in situ monitoring systems that are scalable, interoperable, and standardized, enabling reliable defect detection and control across the diverse landscape of industrial metal AM. To illustrate how these technological directions converge, Figure 9 presents a conceptual framework for next-generation in situ monitoring, showing how multi-modal sensing, real-time data processing, machine learning, and automated feedback can be combined into a unified architecture for both PBF and DED systems.

writing_points.claim: Each defect mechanism (input) produces characteristic physical signals, which are detected by appropriate sensors and translated into engineered features for monitoring and classification (output).
writing_points.source_text: The four principal defect types investigated in Figure 10 originate from various physical mechanisms yet share common process dependencies such as heat input, fluid dynamics, and alloy chemistry. Each defect mechanism (input) produces characteristic physical signals, which are detected by appropriate sensors and translated into engineered features for monitoring and classification (output). This representation clarifies how physical relevance guides sensor selection and data analysis. By linking the underlying physics of defect formation with the corresponding sensor responses and extracted signal features, this conceptual structure highlights how the physical mechanisms of key defects are directly reflected in their measurable sensor signatures. In summary, each defect mechanism produces a characteristic pattern in the sensor data, which makes it possible to distinguish various defect types. The selection of a monitoring method should be done based on the physical origin of the targeted defect, the required detection sensitivity, and the level of process integration. Acoustic emission sensing is suitable for detecting crack initiation due to its sensitivity to elastic wave generation. Optical and thermal imaging systems are more effective for melt pool related defects such as porosity and LoF. Spectroscopic methods are advantageous for identifying compositional anomalies such as non-metallic inclusions.

writing_points.claim: As pores form or approach the surface, they disturb heat flow and reduce local melt pool absorption, which appears as fluctuations in melt pool area, aspect ratio, and surface brightness.
writing_points.source_text: • Cracking caused by thermal gradients, shrinkage strains, and grain boundary liquation produces elastic waves and local heat spikes during crack initiation and propagation. These physical events generate high-frequency acoustic signals, short STFT and CWT bursts, and localized thermal rate fluctuations that can be captured with broadband AE sensors and high-speed thermal imaging. • Process porosity caused by gas entrapment, insufficient melting, or melt pool instability changes the thermal and geometric behavior of the melt pool. As pores form or approach the surface, they disturb heat flow and reduce local melt pool absorption, which appears as fluctuations in melt pool area, aspect ratio, and surface brightness. These surface-level changes can be observed with coaxial imaging, photodiodes, plume monitoring, and low-amplitude acoustic sensors that accompany bubble growth or collapse. • LoF, caused by insufficient energy input or insufficient track overlap, disrupts heat conduction and melt flow. The resulting irregular thermal distribution produces unstable melt pool geometry, fluctuations in emission intensity, local shifts in radiance, and characteristic surface features such as incomplete hatch filling. These characteristics can be observed using vision-based imaging, thermal sensing, and acoustic signals linked to melt flow instability.

writing_points.claim: • Non-metallic inclusions originating from oxidation, powder contamination, or vapor phase reactions modify the local chemistry and optical properties of the melt pool.
writing_points.source_text: • Non-metallic inclusions originating from oxidation, powder contamination, or vapor phase reactions modify the local chemistry and optical properties of the melt pool. These changes influence the emitted light through altered emissivity, modified elemental line ratios, and localized bright spot contrasts. As a result, inclusions can be detected as distinct intensity variations in hyperspectral or multichannel optical data.

selected_writing_points.source_text: On the other hand, time-series sensing has seen rapid growth, with piezoelectric or optical microphones placed on the build or in the chamber to record transient phenomena that resulted in LoF, or crack initiation. For time-series data, features can be organized by representation domain (see Table 1). Time domain statistics (e.g., mean, Root Mean Square (RMS), variance, rise time, and counts) are computationally suitable for online defect detection. Frequency domain data (e.g., spectral centroid, Spectral Skewness) measure characteristics based on various mechanisms. Time–frequency analysis methods, such as the Short Time Fourier Transform (STFT) and Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT), capture how the spectral content of a signal evolves, making them effective for identifying rapid events or overlapping events that arise during AM processing. In parallel, cepstral representations, notably Mel Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCCs), give a compact characterization of the signal’s spectral shape and are frequently used as stable inputs for ML models. Therefore, sensor-based feature extraction in metal AM can be broadly categorized according to the analysis domain. Time domain features are widely applied for real-time detection of amplitude-based anomalies such as spatter bursts or crack initiation, owing to their simplicity and low computational cost [59,60]. Frequency domain features, often obtained through Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) or power-spectral-density analysis, enable defect differentiation by capturing frequency-dependent signatures associated with keyhole collapse, porosity, or process instabilities [26,43,61]. selected_writing_points.claim: Metal additive manufacturing (AM) facilitates the production of geometrically complex components, yet its broader industrial use remains limited by the risk of defect formation and uncertainties in their detection, originating from the highly dynamic and hightemperature process environment.
selected_writing_points.source_text: Metal additive manufacturing (AM) facilitates the production of geometrically complex components, yet its broader industrial use remains limited by the risk of defect formation and uncertainties in their detection, originating from the highly dynamic and hightemperature process environment. To make additive manufacturing more reliable and establish high-quality parts, it is important to understand how these defects form and how their characteristics appear during the process. This review explains the main causes of common defects, such as cracking, porosity, lack of fusion, and inclusions in metal AM processes, including Powder Bed Fusion and Directed Energy Deposition. It also connects main defect formation mechanisms to the optical, thermal, acoustic, and spectroscopic signals that can be measured during the process. Moreover, it is described how commonly used in situ monitoring systems work and how their signals correspond to melt pool dynamics, vapor plume, particle movement, and the solidification process for each kind of defect. An overview is provided of how data from these systems are analyzed, including the extraction of features from images, the evaluation of temperature fields, and the use of time and frequency domain techniques for various signals. By linking the physics of defect formation to measurable process signals, the interpretation of sensor data is enabled, and potential strategies for monitoring specific problems are outlined. Finally, recent developments are examined, including the integration of multiple sensors, advanced feature-representation approaches, and real-time data interpretation coupled with adaptive control. Together, these directions represent promising advances towards more intelligent and reliable monitoring systems for the future of metal AM.
Keywords: directed energy deposition; powder bed fusion; imperfections; cracking; porosity; inclusions; inline monitoring

This review explains the main causes of common defects, such as cracking, porosity, lack of fusion, and inclusions in metal AM processes, including Powder Bed Fusion and Directed Energy Deposition. It also connects main defect formation mechanisms to the optical, thermal, acoustic, and spectroscopic signals that can be measured during the process. Moreover, it is described how commonly used in situ monitoring systems work and how their signals correspond to melt pool dynamics, vapor plume, particle movement, and the solidification process for each kind of defect. An overview is provided of how data from these systems are analyzed, including the extraction of features from images, the evaluation of temperature fields, and the use of time and frequency domain techniques for various signals. By linking the physics of defect formation to measurable process signals, the interpretation of sensor data is enabled, and potential strategies for monitoring specific problems are outlined. Finally, recent developments are examined, including the integration of multiple sensors, advanced feature-representation approaches, and real-time data interpretation coupled with adaptive control. Together, these directions represent promising advances towards more intelligent and reliable monitoring systems for the future of metal AM.

writing_points.claim: Therefore, sensor-based feature extraction in metal AM can be broadly categorized according to the analysis domain.
writing_points.source_text: On the other hand, time-series sensing has seen rapid growth, with piezoelectric or optical microphones placed on the build or in the chamber to record transient phenomena that resulted in LoF, or crack initiation. For time-series data, features can be organized by representation domain (see Table 1). Time domain statistics (e.g., mean, Root Mean Square (RMS), variance, rise time, and counts) are computationally suitable for online defect detection. Frequency domain data (e.g., spectral centroid, Spectral Skewness) measure characteristics based on various mechanisms. Time–frequency analysis methods, such as the Short Time Fourier Transform (STFT) and Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT), capture how the spectral content of a signal evolves, making them effective for identifying rapid events or overlapping events that arise during AM processing. In parallel, cepstral representations, notably Mel Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCCs), give a compact characterization of the signal’s spectral shape and are frequently used as stable inputs for ML models. Therefore, sensor-based feature extraction in metal AM can be broadly categorized according to the analysis domain. Time domain features are widely applied for real-time detection of amplitude-based anomalies such as spatter bursts or crack initiation, owing to their simplicity and low computational cost [59,60]. Frequency domain features, often obtained through Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) or power-spectral-density analysis, enable defect differentiation by capturing frequency-dependent signatures associated with keyhole collapse, porosity, or process instabilities [26,43,61].
